description: Maturity mismatch is the primary structural source of financial fragility — borrowing short and lending long creates vulnerability to funding runs.
Maturity mismatch makes banks vulnerable to runs because short-term creditors can withdraw faster than long-term assets can be liquidated.
The risks of maturity mismatch motivate banks to hold reserves, secondary reserves, and maintain access to borrowing facilities.
Structural fragility in the financial system motivates central banks to develop crisis response tools — both traditional and non-traditional.
The fragility of the financial system motivates capital regulation as a way to require banks to hold loss-absorbing buffers.
Financial fragility exposed by the 2008 crisis motivated structural reforms (Volcker, Vickers, Liikanen) to separate risky from essential banking activities.
The destructive potential of bank runs motivates deposit insurance, which in turn creates moral hazard — a fundamental tension in banking regulation.
Modern wholesale funding runs motivated central banks to develop new facilities (TAF, PDCF, BTFP) to provide liquidity beyond traditional deposit insurance.
Deposit insurance moral hazard motivates capital requirements — capital acts as a 'deductible' ensuring bank owners have skin in the game.
Tension between preventing runs (deposit insurance) and maintaining market discipline (allowing some run risk). Eliminating all run risk destroys market discipline.
Shadow banking's growth (10% to 60% of financial liabilities) created long, opaque intermediation chains that amplified financial fragility.
Shadow banking runs manifest as wholesale funding freezes — ABCP markets, repo markets, and money market funds 'breaking the buck.'
Capital regulation (Basel III Endgame) may push lending to less-regulated private credit, creating new systemic risks — the Eastland effect.
Higher bank capital requirements drive regulatory arbitrage: lending migrates from regulated banks to unregulated private credit markets.
The Volcker Rule reduced dealer inventories, worsening market liquidity and contributing to Treasury market dysfunction in 2014 and 2020.
Understanding Glass-Steagall's original rationale and its critique is prerequisite for evaluating modern structural reform proposals.
The conflict between commercial and investment banking directly parallels the audit-consulting conflict at Big Four accounting firms.
If banks are special because of information from relationships, separating commercial from investment banking (Glass-Steagall) may destroy valuable information flows.
Banks' special monitoring and certification abilities drive financial development, which in turn promotes economic growth (up to the Arcand threshold).
Financial development promotes growth (King-Levine) but 'too much finance' (Arcand et al.) can increase fragility — the inverted-U relationship.
Too-big-to-fail creates an implicit government guarantee that extends moral hazard beyond insured depositors to all creditors of large banks.
The too-big-to-fail problem motivates structural reform proposals that attempt to separate essential from risky banking activities.
Removal of geographic restrictions (Riegle-Neal 1994) enabled bank consolidation, contributing to the creation of too-big-to-fail institutions.
Supply chains mirror financial system fragility: long chains (supplier's supplier = counterparty's counterparty), JIT = low buffers, chokepoints = common factor risk.
Supply chain disruptions parallel bank runs: sudden withdrawal of orders (like chip orders during COVID) mirrors sudden withdrawal of funding.
Understanding money's three functions (store of value, medium of exchange, unit of account) is prerequisite for evaluating Bitcoin and CBDCs.
CBDCs create tension: if people can hold money directly at the central bank, they may withdraw from commercial banks, reducing deposits and potentially causing disintermediation.
ECB, BoJ, and PBoC each developed crisis response tools parallel to but distinct from the Fed's, shaped by their different financial system structures.
PBoC's policy evolution leads to digital yuan (e-CNY) development as part of the transition from quantity-based to modern price-based monetary policy.
The four-decade decline in rates encouraged banks to extend duration (buy longer-term bonds) to maintain yields, amplifying maturity mismatch risk — as SVB demonstrated when rates reversed.
Persistently low rates encouraged reach-for-yield behavior, compressed risk premiums, and inflated asset prices — building fragility that materialized when rates rose.
Private credit mirrors pre-2008 shadow banking: opacity, reliance on ratings, liquidity illusion, and growth outside the regulatory perimeter.
Private credit's growth is an unintended consequence of post-2008 bank regulation — the financial system's Eastland effect.
Tension between making banks safer (higher capital) and pushing risk to less-regulated sectors — stronger banks but potentially a weaker system.
When bank liquidity management tools are exhausted (reserves depleted, markets frozen), central bank crisis tools become necessary.
Banks that optimize liquidity management (minimizing idle reserves) are more profitable but more vulnerable to runs when funding markets freeze.
UK operational separation of audit/consulting parallels Vickers ring-fencing; EY Everest parallels full Glass-Steagall separation; PwC parallels market self-regulation.
If banks are special because of their information advantages, structural reforms that limit their activities may destroy the very features that make them valuable.
The shadow banking system's evolution continues: post-2008 regulation of traditional shadow banking pushed activity into private credit — the new frontier of unregulated intermediation.
Maturity mismatch creates interest rate risk that capital regulation should (but doesn't always) capture — SVB's EVE of -27.7% was not required to be disclosed.
CBDCs and crypto are modern manifestations of the eternal question: what makes something money? Evaluated against store of value, medium of exchange, unit of account.
Aggressive central bank crisis responses (BTFP, systemic risk exception) expand the safety net but also expand moral hazard — Diamond-Rajan vs. Bernanke debate.
